meta.note: Structured spoken-French grammar spine derived from docs/teaching-charter.md. Contemporary spoken register only. Bridge = Spanish. Example clips have no audio ids yet; they get generated when each becomes a lesson.

tense_system.use: now, general truths, near future in context

example.fr: Il faisait beau et j'étais fatiguée.
example.es: Hacía buen tiempo y estaba cansada.
example.en: It was nice out and I was tired.

tense_system.use: plans, predictions; less frequent in speech than futur proche
literary narration; she READS it, never says it

past_two_tense_rule.principle: Spoken past = passé composé (completed events) + imparfait (background/habit/state). Maps onto her Spanish pretérito/imperfecto instinct.

sequence_example.fr: On est arrivés, on a mangé, puis on est partis.
sequence_example.es: Llegamos, comimos, y luego nos fuimos.
sequence_example.note: chain of completed events -> all passé composé

subjunctive.heuristic: Main clause = pressure / wish / feeling / doubt / purpose -> subordinate verb goes subjunctive.

example.fr: Je veux que tu sois là.
example.en: I want you to be there.
I'd like us to leave early.
It's important that we leave early.

example.fr: Je suis content qu'elle soit venue.
Avant qu'on parte, il faut acheter de l'eau.
example.es: Antes de que salgamos, hay que comprar agua.
example.en: Before we leave, we need to buy water.

example.fr: Je t'appelle pour que tu saches.
Bien qu'il soit fatigué, il vient.
Même s'il est fatigué, il vient.
example.en: Even if he's tired, he's coming.

past_subjunctive.name: passé du subjonctif
past_subjunctive.form: avoir/être (present subjunctive) + past participle — COMPOUND, not the imperfect subjunctive
past_subjunctive.use: when the trigger points at something genuinely past

examples.fr: Je suis content que tu sois venu.
C'est dommage qu'on ait raté le train.
examples.es: Es una pena que hayamos perdido el tren.
examples.en: It's too bad we missed the train.

examples.fr: Je ne crois pas qu'il ait compris.

polite_conditional.principle: For polite requests French uses the CONDITIONAL, not a 'quisiera-style' imperfect subjunctive. These are survival phrases for Nice — teach as fixed chunks.

frames.en: I'd like to reserve a table.

frames.fr: Est-ce que je pourrais avoir l'addition ?

spanish_traps.her_instinct: Spanish uses the imperfect subjunctive 'quisiera' as a polite 'I'd like'.
spanish_traps.french_reality: French has NO 'quisiera'-style imperfect subjunctive for this. Polite requests use the conditional: je voudrais, j'aimerais, je pourrais, est-ce que vous pourriez…?
spanish_traps.wrong: (imperfect-subjunctive request forms do not exist in spoken French)
spanish_traps.rule: Map quisiera/querría/me gustaría -> je voudrais / j'aimerais. Never build a French imperfect-subjunctive polite form.

spanish_traps.her_instinct: Spanish requires past subjunctive after a past main verb (Quería que vinieras).
spanish_traps.french_reality: Spoken French keeps the PRESENT subjunctive even after a past main verb.
spanish_traps.rule: After a past main verb, still use the PRESENT subjunctive. The imparfait du subjonctif (vinsses, vînt, fût) is recognition-only.

spanish_traps.french_reality: French has ONE verb être for both.

right.fr: Je suis américaine. / Je suis à Nice.
right.en: I am American. / I am in Nice.

spanish_traps.her_instinct: Spanish uses both simple future (comeré) and ir a + inf (voy a comer).
spanish_traps.french_reality: Spoken French strongly prefers futur proche (je vais manger) over the simple future.
Careful/literary French inserts a meaningless 'ne' after craindre / avant que (Je crains qu'il ne parte). Spoken French usually drops it.
spanish_traps.rule: recognition-only; do not drill production of ne explétif

spanish_traps.her_instinct: Spanish negates with one word: « No veo nada ».
spanish_traps.french_reality: French wraps the verb: ne … rien. Don't add « pas » on top of « rien/jamais/personne ».
spanish_traps.rule: rien/jamais/personne already carry the negation — no extra « pas ».

spanish_traps.her_instinct: Good news: like Spanish, the object pronoun goes BEFORE the verb.
spanish_traps.french_reality: « Lo veo » = « Je le vois », never « Je vois le ». English word order is the trap here.
spanish_traps.rule: Object pronoun before the conjugated verb, exactly like Spanish.

spanish_traps.french_reality: Don't drop the reflexive pronoun: « me levanto » = « je me lève », not « je lève ».

right.fr: Je me lève à sept heures.

spanish_traps.rule: Keep the reflexive pronoun before the verb: je me lève, tu te lèves.

spanish_traps.her_instinct: To say 'better' I'd build « plus bon » (más bueno).
spanish_traps.french_reality: « bon » is irregular: better = « meilleur ». Never « plus bon ».
spanish_traps.wrong: C'est plus bon que la pizza.
spanish_traps.rule: bon → meilleur (adj), bien → mieux (adv). No « plus bon ».

spanish_traps.her_instinct: « mi amiga » is feminine, so « ma amie ».
spanish_traps.french_reality: Before a vowel, feminine possessives switch to mon/ton/son: « mon amie ».
spanish_traps.rule: Before a vowel sound, use mon/ton/son even for feminine nouns.

spanish_traps.her_instinct: « por qué » and « porque » look like one idea.
spanish_traps.french_reality: Question = pourquoi ; answer = parce que. Two different words.
spanish_traps.rule: pourquoi (why, question) vs parce que (because, answer).

spanish_traps.her_instinct: « dame » attaches the pronoun after — so « donne me »?
spanish_traps.french_reality: Affirmative command attaches the pronoun with a hyphen and uses « moi »: « donne-moi ».
spanish_traps.rule: Affirmative imperative: verb-moi/toi (hyphen), like Spanish « dame ».
